sentences.text: Well, after I came back from the Soviet Union-- or from Russia, whichever name you want to use now, now it's Russia again-- after I came back, I lived with my sister-- with my older sister because my other sister, the one that was married to Doctor Leibowicz.
Also, I was taken, sometimes, to work at the train station of the Lodz ghetto, to work there in unloading [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? visas, ?] all kind of transport that came in with people.
And at the end of 1941, I lived through something that sticks in my mind that I don't think will ever leave my-- my-- my mind.
I was told to go to the train with six of my-- seven of my friends-- we're eight boys-- to unload a new transport that was coming from Vienna.

And we were told-- and briefed, which has never happened before, briefed-- that when the luggage will come off the wagons, we should handle that luggage very carefully and put it down on the ground very carefully.
And we were taken, at that time, to the train station, which was in the ghetto.
It was never a train station.
The ghetto was made into a train station.
And a luxury train came from Vienna.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we see-- when the train stopped, we see people getting off the train.
Some of them were like generals.
Some of them were colonels.
The generals, you knew-- the German generals had red bands on their pants, running down from the waist line all the way down to the bottom.
They were generals.
The colonels, every one of them were a high-ranking officer.
We knew that by their behavior, by their nonchalance, by their uniforms, and by-- by the orders that they were giving us how to handle the luggage.
The scene was really macabre.
There's-- there no other explanation I could give you of-- of that exiting of these people.
Every one of them was a high-ranking officer.
Their wives were dressed to kill.
And then I didn't know what a mink coat was, but now I realize some of them had mink coats, broad tails, [? karakul, ?] expensive [INAUDIBLE].
And they all came.
They got off the train, and they stood in line, like soldiers really should.
And their wives stood separately, and they stood separately.
And we are unloading the luggage and putting the luggage, separately, in a very even line, that they should be able to find their luggage.
Now, he was not a German.
I-- to this day I don't know whether he was-- he could have been Latvian, Lithuanian, or Ukrainian.
And that German picks up some kind of rubber club in his hand and smacked that man across the face, and his teeth actually flew in the air.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he almost fell, but before he fell, he gave him another shot from the other end.
No, we didn't!
We didn't!
They came so well dressed.
The ladies, made up, manicured fingers, beautiful hairdos, hats, fur coats.
I said before, fur coats!
And they were dressed in high-ranking German uniforms and medals that wouldn't fit on my chest.
Every one of them.
And then more German soldiers came, and they started hitting every one of them.
They were hitting them without any pity.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, this story starts, the heartbreaking story.
In the Lodz ghetto, we only had outhouses.
Outhouses had a certain value, because human waste was collected into large barrels.
Each barrel was approximately 50 feet long.
50 feet long.
They were made specially for human waste.
In the ghetto, we called it [? fecalia. ?] And that human waste was taken out of the ghetto for the Polish farmers.
But that barrel was on four wheels, and humans were dragging that barrel.
All of a sudden, we see a few barrels coming, and then-- by the way, they were tied in to the wagons just like horses were.
There were six in the front and two in the back, pushing it.
And six were dragging it.
And those barrels came.
Those people that worked these barrels were told to go home.
And they put those officers, in their uniforms, with their medals into those leather straps.
And they made them drag those wagons into the ghetto.
And the women, immediately, the Germans tore off their coats, tore their hats off.
The women were pushed and beaten.
And they went into a place called [INAUDIBLE], which was a jail.
And they put them there until they were resettled.
They actually didn't want to put them in jail.
I don't think so, because they were freed one by one after they found a place where they should stay.
But what happened the next couple of days, we had about 10 to 20 suicides daily in the ghetto by these people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They just didn't want to live anymore.
They didn't want to live any more indignity.
And about a week later, I was in the cemetery, and some of these bodies showed up.
But when I watched them pull that wagon with that long barrel of human waste, in their uniforms with the red bands, and about five minutes before, they were dressed like kings.
And five minutes later, they were dragging a wagon full of human-- human waste, and it was splashing on them all over.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we took the entire luggage that was there.
That's why I say something macabre, something that it-- it just doesn't happen.
Maybe in some Kafka's stories you could read something that-- that is beyond your comprehension.
We put all this luggage back into the wagons, and the train moved away.
And the luggage was absolutely superb.
And the coats we picked up, the fur coats.
We were told to pick everything up and put it back into the train.
What they pulled off the women.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is something that I don't think I will ever, ever forget.
When that train came, really just like a Kafka story, with-- with the soldiers getting off, officers with dignity and demanding respect, and commanding respect.
All of a sudden, they-- they're dragging wagons of human waste--
--which was splashing all over them.
Dignity?
No.
No, no, no, you couldn't maintain dignity when you're full of blood and full of human waste.
No, no, no.
I have not seen anyone beg.
Maybe when they were put up for overnight until they were resettled to their apartments.
Maybe they begged.
Maybe they asked for something.
But that was not for me to see or to know.
Well then, let's go back to 1942, which is also a very, very memorable time for me.
It started May 4th or 5th-- 4th or 5th-- of 1942, where the Germans called a Shpero.
Shpero, S-H-P-E-R-O.
It was also called, by the Jewish command, it was called resettlement.
That they are going to resettle Jews from the Lodz ghetto into different towns where there are good factories and you'll be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] going to be taken care of.
But what actually happened during the Shpero, they did not take an able man.
They were picking on older people, people that look old, people that looked sick, small children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That-- that was an outright lie, and that happened for about two weeks.
A lie!
They were lying!
It-- it just-- It couldn't be the truth.
It had to be a lie.
If they take people like that--
My wife's mother was taken there, at that time.
They were taking people, putting them on-- on-- on trucks, standing up, and taking them to a different town.
It just didn't seem plausible.
They're going to be resettled, because the Lodz ghetto is going to be an Arbeitscamp, strictly Arbeitscamp.
You know what an Arbeitscamp--
All right.
So-- and then, in the Lodz ghetto, we had buildings that were four and five stories high.
So some people, if they had small children, what they did is they used to hide them in the attic of the fifth floor.
They would never think that the Germans are going to go up and look for people on the fifth floor walk-up.
But they did.
When they knew there was an attic, they went up there.
They looked in the attic.
They looked in the fourth floor.
If they found small children, instead of telling them to go down, they opened up the window and threw them down from the fourth and fifth floor into a truck.
When they hit the truck, they were dead.
Now picture this inhumanity by men, and your blood curdles.
And this you saw with your own eyes.

It's not something that someone told you, because all the able men and women that were going to work had to be downstairs in the yard, and being examined by the Germans, if they're able to work or not.
Just like my wife's mother was not able to work, but she was in the line-up.
She thought maybe she's going to pass the inspection, but she didn't.
I passed inspection.
My wife passed the inspection.
We were still able.
We had a little life in us.
We could work and do something for them.

But to see the children flying through the windows into the trucks with their blood splattered all over the truck, and around the truck, and on the ground, that was something that is really not for a human mind to comprehend.
It certainly was not for my mind to comprehend.
I couldn't.
And that went on and on and on for about two weeks.
And when we heard the word of a Shpero, that a Shpero is coming, our blood curdles.
Our blood became ice.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on about the 22nd or 23rd of May, the same year of 1942, new transports used to come in from western Europe-- from Austria, Czechoslovakia, western Germany-- all of Germany.
They used to come in to the Lodz ghetto to replenish the people that-- that were taken.
I think, if I remember correctly, it was about 35,000 or 40,000 people that were taken away in 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And about 20,000 came back from Germany.
And the funny part of it was that these Jews that came from Germany or from Vienna, some of them were half-Jews, some of them were one-quarter Jews.
And some of them came in with their wives or husbands who were not Jews, but they elected to come with their wife or their husband.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to get ahead of my story, about a year or two later, if the husband or the wife that was Jewish died, then they were able to make an application to go back to Germany once they had proven that they're of true, Aryan race.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And--
Oh, yeah, yeah.
As a matter of fact, I became very good friend with one fellow.
His name was Aaron Vogel.
His father was Jewish, his mother was Christian, And the three of them came into the ghetto.
And he worked with me in the same commando.
And we became very good friends.
Very good friends.
As time went on, the food was getting worse and worse and harder to get.
The bread was not even the same.
The bread started to get, like, gooey, like plaster, soft plaster in some-- some instances.
It depends what kind of bread you got.
Some of them didn't bake through, some- some of them was mixed, or some of them was just made heavier, so that whoever was baking the bread didn't watch it, or they couldn't help themselves.
It-- it did get worse.
The ration of food that you were getting-- every 10 days-- was smaller.
Many a time, you didn't get-- you only got that brown sugar, but the brown sugar was very soft and moist, like it was never worked out [INAUDIBLE].
Listen, if you got it, you were a rich man.
It's only when you didn't get it, you-- you felt bad.
Well, the Judenrat, the people that surrounded or worked for the Judenrat, most of them I had absolutely no respect for.
If anything, I have hate for them.
A lot of these people were so cruel, unnecessary.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They tried to justify their job, so they were cruel.
They did things that they really didn't have to do, far and above and beyond their duty.
However, Rumkowski was surrounded with these people.
Rumkowski was the chief of the Judenrat.
And to my mind-- and I have expressed my mind many a times about this-- this man.
I didn't know him.
I saw him once in a while on the street.
He passed by.
He drove in a dorozka with a horse and a carriage, and with a policeman watching over him.
But I think that, thanks to him, more Jews lived through than any other Jews from the ghetto.
Even though we were all-- with the exception of 840 people that remained in the ghetto-- we went all to Auschwitz.
But thanks to him, some of us-- a small, small number-- but some of us lived through.
Because he kept us alive until September, October of 1944, until he couldn't possibly hold out anymore--
--and the ghetto was closed.
Well, he kept the factories going.
He supplied the Germans with what they needed, with the clothes, free of charge, of course.
With slave labor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In return, he fed the slave labor.
In return, he gave them a place where to live.
I was grateful I was with my sister.
Two sisters I still had in the ghetto.
And we lived next door to each other.
So-- and I was all alone, so they were sort of the-- the-- the tower of my strength, having two sisters and two brother-in-laws.
And I felt, every time they looked at me, I-- I see that love is transposing on me because her daughter, her eight-year-old daughter, was with my parents.
And maybe they're still alive, although they were dead.
[INAUDIBLE] We didn't believe it.
We didn't believe that there is such thing as burning people, killing people.
Shooting people, yes.
But organized burning, organized gassing, or just organized killing, organized murder-- that something like this could exist in a country such as Germany, I didn't believe.
I honestly didn't believe.
I read some-- some of the books.
I read some of the newspapers.
I heard about the book that Hitler wrote when he was in jail in Nuremberg.
In 1923, he was in jail when he wrote I-- I heard some excerpts from his book.
But he only said that he will destroy the Jewish race as a nation.
He never talked about burning.
The only time I heard about burning Jews was in the 7th century, when Martin Luther, in Germany, read a book- wrote the book about destroying the infidels.
In the 17th-- what  did I say, 19th?
Oh, in the 17th century, Martin Luther advocated total and complete destruction of Jewish nation.
And some of these excerpts found themselves in which Hitler used.
But it was also something unbelievable, even if I were to go back to the 15th century, of the Inquisition.
So there was a choice.
If you wanted to become a Christian, you became a Marrano, and you lived.
But if you didn't want to give up your belief, they burnt you at the stake, or they just send you out from Spain by order of the Pope.
I have to throw in that word because it's in me.
I'm sorry.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
In Auschwitz, I-- I only exchanged one.
In Auschwitz, when they took away my clothes, they probably found it.
They were never used, no.
No, no, no.
In the ghetto, I was afraid to use them because, unfortunately, among some of our people, you had informants.
And there's another story with that--
--which I was also involved with my wife's oldest brother.
It seemed that my father had a friend.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this friend had become an informant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it could have been that my father told him something.
It could've been that he knew of it.
No, a mangle is something to iron the wash.
No, no, no, no, no.
That's something else.
A mangle is after the clothes is dry.
In Europe, we didn't iron the clothes most of the time.
Especially if you made a heavy wash.
Linen you didn't iron.
Flat things you didn't iron.
After the wash was done, they used to take the entire wash into a mangle.
It's a very heavy-- like a wagon.
Inside, heavy rocks.
Underneath, you have two circles of wood.
You put the clothes underneath, and you turn it around, going back and forth, until the clothes is very flat, like iron.
That's a mangle.
It never occurred to me that the room is smaller.
I just realized that the room is not the same.
And I asked my sister.
My sister didn't tell me anything.
I don't know.
She knew.
I don't know.
Now, that informant is dead.
We killed him in Auschwitz.
We, I mean, other people who had something against him.
The minute he arrived in Auschwitz, just like everyone else, we killed him.
He informed on me in the Kripo-- K-R-I-P-O-- Kripo.
Kripo is short for Kriminalpolizei.
Kripo.
Kriminalpolizei.
They had a department in the ghetto, and they used to call in people who they suspected, or who were informed, that they know of a certain treasure that is hidden.
That either they hid it or their parents hid it, and they took you in there and gave you hearings.
Every day, a hearing was beating to the death.
My wife's brother was called in, and I think that's what killed him.
They beat the heck out of him for only one week.
I was there for three weeks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had approximately [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 12 to 15 hearings.
And every hearing was complete and total torture.
Torturous beating.
And I really didn't know that my parents hid anything.
Finally, three weeks later, they let me out.
And I was 3/4 dead.
Believe me, I was 3/4 dead, and alive.
And somehow, I came through it.
And about 10 months later, the Kripo came to my aunt's sister's house that had the mangle on the ground floor-- it's sort of half a basement-- and they go right in there without questions.
They break the wall and sure enough, they take out stuff that I realized belonged to us.
Which means that my father did hide something in there.
But I didn't know.
I just didn't realize.
Yeah, but they called me to the Kripo.
They didn't call my sister, because my sister's name was not Berkenwald anymore.
It was Kupermince.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the informant said, if you call his son here, we'll know for sure.
The only thing that the informant didn't know is that, when my parents were taken out, I wasn't there.
She said, no, nothing happened.
